data.title: Realty Income: Macro Pessimism Triggers Discounted Valuations & Richer Dividend Yields
data.description:
I previously covered Realty Income (O) in November 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, thanks to the richer dividend yields arising from the sideways stock price trading, the consistently raised payouts, and the REIT's ongoing diversification to the EU investment market.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the O stock here, thanks to the improved risk/reward from the recent selloff and the consequently discounted valuations/richer dividend yields.

My optimism is attributed to the management's promising FY2026 guidance, the healthy balance sheet, and the great insights from the uptrend support line established since late 2023, with their REIT operations likely to remain resilient despite the uncertain macroeconomic environment entering FY2026.

O's Selloff Has Been Unwarranted

Since my last Buy rating, O has boasted an excellent total return of +9.3% compared to the wider market at -5%, with a similar trend also observed in its REIT peers in varying degrees, despite the waning market rotation momentum from early 2026.

The recent correction is unsurprising indeed, since its REIT operations are likely to be impacted by the likely to remain elevated inflationary/interest rate environment.

For now, I am of the opinion that the recent correction has been unwarranted, albeit being a boon for those looking to add, since it has brought the O stock nearer to its uptrend support line established since late 2023, especially given the relatively robust FQ4'25 performance metrics and the promising FY2026 guidance.

For example, the REIT has reported a robust investment volume of $6.3B in FY2025 (+61.5% YoY) at a somewhat stable initial weighted average cash yield of 7.3% in FY2025 (-0.1 points YoY), with it likely to trigger the expanded investment spreads against the moderating weighted average interest rate of 3.93% in FY2025 (-0.14 points YoY).

Combined the effect of the Fed's prior rate cuts in late 2025, I can understand why O has felt confident enough to reiterate their ability to deliver another year of excellent investment spread in FY2026, with it building upon their historical Weighted Average Investment Spreads of ~150bps since 1995.

This is especially since the REIT's excellent assets have been able to command the higher rental recapture of 104.9% in FQ4'25 and 103.9% in FY2025, compared to the average of 103.2% reported between 1996 and 2025.

This is on top of their richer rent growth on new tenant placement at 127% in FY2025, compared to the 88% reported between 2013 and 2019 and 111% reported between 2020 and 2025 - all of which underscoring the REIT's portfolio resilience despite the challenging macroeconomic environment thus far.

As a result, while the market has decided to de-risk first and ask questions later, as observed in O's recent correction by -10.6% from the 52 week highs and the iShares Global REIT ETF's (REET) similarly steep selloff by -10%, I am not overly concerned about O's prospects entering the challenging FY2026.

This is attributed to:

  • the robust tenant demand for their portfolio as observed in the rich rental recapture/rent growth, as discussed above,
  • the well diversified industry exposure with "~91% of portfolio operate non-discretionary, service-oriented retail, or low-price-point businesses—resilient to economic cycles," and
  • the minimal tenant concentration, with the largest 5 tenants only comprising 14.3% of Total Annualized Base Rent.

The same profitable growth prospects have also been observed in O's promising FY2026 investment volume guidance at $8B (+26.9% YoY) and FY2026 AFFO per share guidance of $4.40 at the midpoint (+2.8% YoY), with it building upon the FY2025 outperformance at $6.3B (+61.5% YoY) and $4.28 (+2.1% YoY), respectively.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have raised their forward estimates, with the REIT expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +9.1%/+3.2% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +28.4%/+4.8% and a 10Y CAGR of +18.7%/+4.6%, respectively, with it underscoring why O's recent selloff has been mostly sentiment driven, instead of fundamentals.

Discounted Valuations & Richer Dividends

Thanks to the -10.6% correction from the recent highs and the sideways trading since June 2020, I am of the opinion that O remains compellingly valued at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 13.80x, down from the recent highs of 15.35x, the 5Y mean of 15.24x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 19.27x.

A similar conclusion may also be derived when comparing O's FWD Price/FFO valuations of 13.58x, the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 19.27x, and the consensus FFO per share growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +3.9% to its REIT peers, including:

  • Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) at 16.46x/14.61x/+4.5%, and
  • Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) at 12.63x/17.39x/+4.6%, respectively,

with it apparent that O's profitable growth trends remain reasonably valued here, aside from W. P. Carey (WPC) at 12.83x/14.91x/+12.5%.

Thanks to the recently raised dividends by +0.2% and the 5Y dividend growth rate at +3.53%, I believe that O's dividend investment thesis remains extremely compelling at forward dividend yield of 5.35%, compared to the 5Y averages of 4.92% and the 4Y pre-pandemic averages of 3.98%.

Given the sector wide pessimism, it is unsurprising that O's peers also boast compelling dividend income prospects, as observed in:

  • GTY at 6.11% against the 5Y averages of 5.69%,
  • ADC at 4.23%/4.19%, and
  • WPC at 5.50%/5.77%, respectively.

O's balance sheet remains healthy at a Net Debt to Annualized Pro Forma Adjusted EBITDAre of 5.4x in FQ4'25 as well, not too far from its REIT peers, including GTY at 5.1x, ADC at 3.8x, and WPC at 5.6x, with it apparent that the former remains well capitalized to embark on the aggressive investment volumes entering FY2026.

This is especially since they have opportunistically divested numerous properties in FY2025, including those with "higher-risk locations," "Chapter 11" risks, and "credit and renewal risk," to "enhancing portfolio quality and redeploying capital into higher return opportunities."

These underscore their competent risk management and their ability to raise capital across dilutive ATM means, debt, and asset divestitures.

If anything, O has also opportunistically repurchase shares in Q1'26, with the aim of reducing the share dilution impact from their first convertible note offering of $862M, with it underscoring the management's focus on shareholder returns beyond dividend payouts.

O Is An Even Better Buy Here

For now, O has notably returned much of their January/February 2026 gains, with the stock now retesting the support levels and the 100/200 day moving averages of $60s by the time of writing.

Based on the reversing momentum and the consequently oversold technical indicators/RSIs/trading volumes by the time of writing, I am of the opinion that O is likely to be well supported along its uptrend support line established since late 2023.

My optimism is attributed to the stock already trading near my base-case fair value estimates of $59.00, based on the FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 13.80x and the FY2025 AFFO per share performance at $4.28 (+2.1% YoY).

In the intermediate term, I believe that O is likely to continue trading sideways for so long that the macroeconomic outlook remains uncertain, as the Fed also expects the core PCE inflation to moderate to 2% only by 2028, with it implying a prolonged Fed rate cut cadence.

The same has been observed in my base-case long-term price target of $64.60, based on the FWD Price/FFO valuations of 13.58x and the consensus FY2028 FFO per share estimates of $4.76.

On the other hand, it goes without saying that O is unlikely to remain discounted compared to its historical trends, with the eventual normalization in macroeconomic environment, the consequently lower cost borrowing environment, and the expanded investment spreads (assuming similar investment cash yields) likely to trigger an upward re-rating in its future valuations.

Based on the speculative upgrading to the REIT's historical 5Y pre-pandemic Price/FFO valuation mean of 19.27x, there appears to be a rich upside potential of +51% to my bull-case long-term price target of $91.70.

Pending that event, O investors may sit back and enjoy the rich, monthly incomes at yields of over >5%, with it implying its excellent inflation hedge story against the global inflation average at 4.2% in 2025, ~4.3% over the past ten years, and ~4.3% over the past twenty years.

This is especially since their dividends remain secure at AFFO payout ratio of 74.90%, somewhat inline with the sector median of 74.43%.

As a result of the improved risk/reward from the recent selloff, the consequently discounted valuations/richer dividend yields, the management's promising FY2026 guidance, the healthy balance sheet, and the great insights from the uptrend support line established since late 2023, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the O stock here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Why Is Gold Crashing During War? 3 Reasons I'm Buying The Selloff
data.description:
Umbrella

As the world watched bombs fall in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz be closed off in March 2026 and oil prices soar past $100 per barrel, many investors were shocked to see the ultimate safe-haven suffer a significant decline (greater than 15%) as they had come to view gold as their insurance policy from geopolitical uncertainty. It's a real paradox for many investors, who believe the yellow metal behaved like an "umbrella" in case of rain. But there are many different types of "rain," and I believe the underlying structure of the global financial system needs to be properly explained, clearly, objectively, and far removed from opinion.

Gold has not failed, it is the investor's expectations that are wrongfully being driven by an incorrect model. Gold does not always protect investors during war-driven liquidity shocks; it tends to protect better against monetary debasement and a loss of confidence in fiat regimes. The type of inflation being created in this war is a type of inflation that comes from the supply side (and not the demand side, like the post-Covid period), triggering a market reaction that is unfavorable to gold.

This article presents three key explanations for why there was a sell-off of gold during this time period, the difference between monetary devaluation and inflation, the nature of liquidation, and how high real yields and opportunity cost damage gold.

Monetary Debasement

To understand why Gold is down heading into War, we need to first understand how to tell the difference between a Devaluation of Currency (a deliberate reduction in the real value of money, often associated with monetary expansion or debasement), versus inflation. It has been easy for many people to confuse inflation with devaluation.

A debasement, in theory, is a method that reduces the value of the currency by design. That occurs when a country's government cannot meet its financial obligations via taxation. Therefore, they attempt to "devalue" the real value of the currency as a method to help meet their budgetary deficits indirectly.

One of the earliest and best-known examples of debasement comes from ancient Rome. In that time, Roman Emperor Nero had caused several changes in the monetary system in response to the Great Fire of Rome, which burned much of Rome to the ground. He decreased the percentage of pure Silver in the Denarius (the coin used by Romans) from 98 percent to 90 percent. Additionally, he altered the Denarius' weight. With the same amount of precious metal being produced, Nero now had more Denarii available to him to circulate. He would use this additional supply of coins as a way to generate revenue without having to raise taxes directly.

Over the years, debasements continued. With each successive century, the quality of the Denarius was further diminished. During Marcus Aurelius’ reign, the Denarius had even lower quality. Then during Septimius Severus, the Denarius experienced a near 50 percent loss of purity. By the time of Gallienus, the Denarius contained nearly nothing of actual value. It looked like a Silver Coin, due to its coloration, yet it had less than five percent real Silver. All the remainder was made from Base Metals covered very thinly with a thin coating of Silver to maintain the outward appearance.

Thanks to debasement, and returning to the present day, the state is able to spend more by creating more money and putting it into circulation, while those who hold that money see their purchasing power decrease. At first, the process may seem invisible, but over time it manifests itself in higher prices, a loss of confidence in the currency, and the real impoverishment of savers.

As I mentioned earlier, debasement is a means whereby a government reduces the real value of money so that they may pay for expenses they were unable to afford through existing revenue streams. Historically, this was done by reducing the amount of Precious Metals within coins. Currently, there are many other ways to accomplish this goal, although the underlying economic principle remains constant: issue “watered down” currency to support an imbalance between tax receipts and outlays.

Feature Monetary Debasement Supply-Shock Inflation
Origin Expansion of the money supply and chronic deficits Geopolitical disruptions and resource shortages
Central Bank Response Accommodative policy (QE, zero rates) Restrictive policy (QT, rate hikes)
Impact on the Dollar Tendency toward depreciation Strengthening due to demand for liquidity
Relationship with Gold Strong positive correlation Often negative correlation in the short term
2026 Scenario Post-election U.S. fiscal policy Middle East conflict and a Hormuz blockade

Currently, the primary manifestations of debasement include quantitative easing (QE) and debt monetization. With U.S. total outstanding public debt now totaling greater than $38 trillion (>100% GDP), along with annualized interest expenditures now projected to exceed $1 trillion in 2026, the fiscal situation in the United States is described by analysts as being under "fiscal dominance." Gold generally responds positively when the market believes that the central bank must print money to either avoid bankruptcy or maintain artificially low interest rates ("financial repression").

However, the current inflationary pressures resulting from the conflict in Iran do not represent debasement, but rather supply side inflation. Due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz, oil prices have exceeded $100 causing a dramatic increase in energy costs that represents a severe recessionary tax on all economies around the world.

The extreme nature of such inflationary pressures results in brutal responses from central banks. Rather than printing additional money (which would favorably impact gold), the Federal Reserve must remove liquidity and/or raise interest rates in order to forestall the development of future inflationary expectations. This was evident when traders' bets on another future interest rate increase by October reached 50% in March 2026; thereby eliminating any hope of accommodating monetary policy.

Mechanisms of Liquidity

The value of Gold also acts as a "safety net" (which I call "the gold-piggy-bank-of-coins") when the system has been hit by a large scale financial disaster in terms of liquidity. Historically, Gold prices have fallen before they reach the apex, in a context of a major market crash.

#1 Trading Program

The reason this occurs is strictly mechanical and therefore can be classified as an investors positioning phenomenon. Each time a large scale macro event hits the world economy, many Institutional Fund Managers will execute what is referred to as a "trading program". Which essentially means the immediate and mechanically forced liquidation of a portion of their risk exposure to create cash/liquidity. In lieu of selling off their holdings selectively, Fund Managers aim to raise a certain percentage of liquidity by reducing a fixed percentage of other portfolio positions. As such, the assets with the greatest appreciation will typically be the first sold. This phenomenon extends throughout the entire market and provides insight on why some assets, despite being positively impacted by an unexpected shock may decline further.

#2 Margin Calls

The second technical aspect involves "margin calls". As stock and bond markets collapse, institutional investors and hedge-funds receive margin calls on their highly-levered portfolios. When under duress, investors do not sell what they wish to sell, but rather what they are able to sell. Gold is among the most liquid assets available globally. And, gold had accrued tremendous gains over the past year (more than +50%) and over the past five years (+150%). Thus, selling gold provides investors with an opportunity to recover some portion of their losses on less liquid or significantly depreciating assets without being required to fire-sale illiquid assets in private-equity or private-credit investments, for example.

To illustrate this behavior, consider the following graph illustrating how gold declined immediately following the collapse of Lehman Brothers in 2008, only to rebound strongly in 2009. We see similar patterns occurring currently.

#3 Bubble-foam

Additionally, the gold market became overcrowded with so-called speculative "bubble-foam" by 2025. Approximately $70 billion was invested in gold-based EFTs that utilized 2X or 3X leverage. All of these ETFs utilize daily-rebalancing: when gold prices fall, the EFT must sell contracts in order to maintain the leverage ratio. However, such activity creates automated-selling pressure that further exacerbates downward price movement.

Therefore, the "alarm-fire" created by this mechanism has resulted in erratic movements over recent days. Buyers appear to have disappeared and gold prices continue to fall-not because confidence in gold has been lost-but due to algorithmic-risk-management-systems closing-out positions at the same time.

Interest Rates

The third cause for the downward trend in gold prices, I believe, has nothing to do with either sentiment or global politics, rather, it relates to mathematical finance. Gold does not produce income in the form of coupon payments or dividend distributions. Therefore, the relative attractiveness of gold is heavily dependent upon whether investors can find alternative investments (with acceptable levels of risk) which provide greater benefits. For example, when real yields are low, the costs associated with investing in gold are so low that they essentially make gold a viable competitor to other stores of value. However, when 10-year U.S. Treasury yields move into the 4.25%-4.35% area, and especially when 10-year real yields move higher, the opportunity cost of holding gold rises sharply. At such time, gold becomes an overly expensive investment in comparison to alternatives since investors must forego the potential for a tangible and liquid investment that carries no theoretical credit risk.

PIMCO's way of evaluating gold was helpful to me and I have outlined below, in simple terms, how they evaluate gold. In addition to viewing gold as a commodity or a safe haven, we can also view gold as a financial asset which does not pay income and has a "long-term," "real," duration. The PIMCO researchers concluded that over the long-term most of the price movement of gold could be attributed to changes in 10-year real yields. The theory underlying this concept is simply stated: If the investors' market requires a higher real rate of return on their investment in safe investments (TIPS/Treasury), then the price of gold will decline so that it remains competitive with other safe investments for the allocation of capital.

To quantify this relationship, PIMCO empirically estimated gold's 'real duration' at approximately eighteen years. It is important to understand gold's sensitivity to interest rates based upon gold's "real duration." As you can see, gold is extremely sensitive to both nominal and real interest rates. A one hundred basis points increase in real yields has historically led to a large contraction in gold prices when adjusted for inflation.

To me, I also believe that this is another aspect of gold that many investors have misunderstood in recent years. During the period spanning 2022 through 2025, the traditional relationship between gold and real yields became somewhat distorted. Many believed that the older model no longer applied, primarily due to central bank purchases and geopolitical support for gold prices even when nominal interest rates were increasing. However, that was probably the exception and not the rule. When markets experience increased uncertainty, decreased liquidity and heightened margin financial demand begins to take precedence again, markets tend to revert back to the prior valuation anchors. One of those anchors for gold is still the level of real yields.

Therefore, in my opinion, the current downturn in gold prices should not be viewed solely as a result of profit taking and/or declining demand for safe-haven assets. Instead, it should be viewed as a revision of opportunity costs. If an investor can now place capital in U.S. government bonds earning greater than 4%, while having an infinitely more stable risk profile than gold, then gold must represent some additional benefit to be included within an investor's portfolio. If gold cannot represent those benefits via momentum, immediate protection, or future expectations of further devaluation of currency by central banks, then gold will adjust accordingly, and will do so aggressively.

At this juncture in time, gold is not being viewed as a monetary relic that exists outside of the financial universe but rather as an asset with very high real duration and thus extremely vulnerable to a “higher for longer” interest rate environment. That is the crux. As long as the market continues to assign high real yields to investments, sustaining ownership positions in gold will continue to become increasingly more difficult to sustain. And that alone can account for a large portion of the decline currently underway.

The 3 Phase Framework: The Investor's Compass

Personally, I believe the current market selloff shouldn't be seen as an anomalous event or as a farewell to bullish sentiment. Rather, it's part of a mechanism that has been seen many times in market history. Indeed, when a market shock occurs or a system in general comes under stress, safe-haven assets are first bought for protection, then sold out of necessity, and only later do they return to their true role. Therefore, in my opinion, we should be talking about a "liquidity crisis" and not a value crisis.

For me, there are three phases.

Phase 1 (2024 – early 2026)

In the first phase, gold rises due to the arguments we made earlier (high deficits, growing debt, the risk of long-term monetary debasement, and a gradual loss of confidence in the real solidity of fiat currencies). Added to this are record purchases by central banks of emerging markets, particularly China, India, Turkey, and Poland, which have purchased tons annually since 2022. Among the reasons, I can certainly cite the freezing of Russian reserves, which prompted other central banks and governments to purchase gold.

Phase 2 (present day – March 2026)

This Phase is the current Phase that we are probably going through right now. The catalysts for this Phase are an extremely adverse external shock—war, an oil price shock, extreme market volatility, high interest rates, and a very strong US Dollar—will dramatically alter how gold is used in investor portfolios. By all logic, gold would be the first beneficiary of pandemonium. However, as investors experience extreme levels of anxiety (during which time gold acts as the 'cash ATM' of the financial system) it usually has the exact opposite effect on gold. When margin calls escalate and money managers have to convert their positions into realizable funds immediately, they do not convert their positions with those items that are either illiquid at the moment or those items which are significantly devalued. Instead, managers will attempt to convert their positions to those items which remain profitable, liquid and can be converted to cash quickly. Gold virtually always falls into each of these categories.

Historical Examples:

  • 2008: Collapse from $1,000 to $720 (Lehman Brothers).
  • 2020: Collapse from $1,683 to $1,474 in 10 days (Covid).
  • 2022: After the Russian invasion of Ukraine, gold rose briefly, then fell due to forced liquidations and the strengthening of the dollar.
  • 1983: The gold price collapsed by over $100, caused by massive OPEC sell-offs due to a lack of oil revenues.

Phase 3: the explosive recovery (next phase)

At this time the overall economic system has become overwhelmed with the burden of unstable markets, high interest payments from increasing debt, and restrictive banking regulations. Here the central banks have to reverse course on their commitment to monetary constraint and will lower rates, provide liquid funds back into the economy, and some type of monetary assistance is provided (either explicitly or implicitly). In doing so, gold's role as a funding tool for the system transitions to being viewed once again as a safe haven investment. Again, here the story is relatively simple. Gold after selling off over 30% in 2008 did not just recover; it climbed 165% and reached a price level of $1,921 in 2011. Similarly, gold quickly bounced back after the covid-19 crash, rapidly returning to historical price highs. Therefore, if a gold "crash" occurs due to an inability to fund the market rather than a failure of the underlying economics, it typically represents the midpoint of the cycle rather than its final destination.

Conclusion

We are not witnessing a collapse of gold as an asset, instead, we have a temporary halt. We know from history (2008 and 2020) that mechanical panic occurs in Phase 2 creating the greatest opportunity for investors who are able to see past short-term volatility.

I'm still quite hopeful about gold. Personally, I don't see gold as something you keep in your portfolio forever. However, I believe that it can be cleverly used both as a tactical instrument and for risk management purposes. Gold should comprise no less than 5 percent nor exceed 15 percent of your total investment portfolio. If gold exceeds 15 percent of your total investment portfolio then you will run into unacceptable concentration risk. Currently, I’m using the recent decline in gold prices to increase my ownership in gold. Presently, I own approximately 5 percent of gold in my portfolio. However, I will buy additional shares of gold over time, as the prices of gold continue to fall.

When does Phase 3 occur? Gold has historically recovered as soon as the financial markets realize that the need for immediate liquidity diminishes. The critical event which will lead to Phase 3 is when the Strait of Hormuz is reopened or there is evidence of some form of de-escalation in the conflict between the U.S. and Iran. Once oil begins flowing again, the need for all of the Gulf countries to liquidate their gold reserves to obtain currency will immediately disappear. Subsequently, the focus of the marketplace will shift back toward the only remaining mathematically verifiable fact related to gold — the $38 trillion of U.S. debt and the structural necessity to devalue the dollar if the U.S. wishes to achieve a level of sustainability with regard to the U.S. debt burden.

data.title: Weibo Corporation: Still Long Even Though Headwinds Have Gained Strength
data.description:
Weibo Corporation (WB), a social media platform in China, has not done well recently, despite the release of the latest earnings report during this time. The Q4 FY2025 report came in below expectations, which caused the stock to drop and pushed it below a support level that had kept WB afloat for months. However, while headwinds have gotten stronger for several reasons, there are nonetheless plenty of reasons to be long WB.

WB fell below a key support level

A previous article from January 2026 written by myself mentioned why WB had enough to take it higher. For instance, the stock had rebounded after a selloff, which did not happen by accident at it occurred close to an important support level, around $9.50 or so. WB appeared to be heading higher by resuming the prior uptrend after a temporary correction, which is part of why I chose to rate WB a buy in the article.

However, this did not turn out to be correct because the stock proceeded to challenge the previously mentioned support level once more and while support did manage to repeat what it had done before, which is to prevent WB from going lower, by doing so for a second time, it was unable to do so for a third time. The chart above shows how the stock fell below the support level on March 18 after bouncing off of the support level multiple times.

A new support level seems to have been found

The previous article mentioned how WB had rallied from a low of $7.10 in April 2025 to a high of $12.96 in October 2025 and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $7.10 to $12.96 is $9.34 per ADS. WB challenged this support level a number of times in the past half year and for a time support was able to stand its ground.

For instance, he first challenge ended in November 2025 with WB reaching as low as $9.46, or just $0.12 above $9.34, before turning higher. Another challenge in the early part of March saw the stock hit an intraday low of $9.23 on March 9, which is below $9.34, only to bounce and close the day higher at $9.60, or above $9.34.

However, support at $9.34 did not hold because the stock was able to break below it on March 18 after the release of the latest report, which quickly resulted in a new 2026 low of $8.36 on March 19. WB has been dithering since then with no new lows and there seems to be a reason for this. The next Fibonacci retracement level following the 61.8% level is the 76.4% retracement level and this level seems to have come into play.

The 76.4% retracement of $7.10 to $12.96 is $8.48. This could explain why even though WB hit an intraday low of $8.36 on March 19, which is below $8.48, it bounced to close the day higher at $8.78, or above $8.48. The stock has been hovering above this price point in recent days, but it has not been able to close below $8.48, at least so far. WB closed at $8.58 per ADS on March 27.

All this suggests WB has found another support level. Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that after the recent slide in the stock, WB is now well into oversold territory with an RSI reading in the mid twenties. These conditions, oversold and close to support, could enable WB to regain its footing, perhaps to the point that it can go on another rally.

So while bulls suffered a setback with the loss of an important support level, which had kept the stock afloat for months, bulls can take solace in the fact that WB has found another support level to replace the one previously lost. It is not impossible for WB to break support once again, but the existence of support should provide a certain level of assurance for those thinking of getting in on WB.

A disappointing report breaks support

It’s no coincidence support was breached after the release of the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report. First, WB missed earnings expectations when it announced non-GAAP earnings of $0.25 per ADS, $0.07 below consensus estimates, on revenue of $473.26M in Q4 FY2025. In comparison, WB earned $0.40 per ADS on revenue of $456.83M in Q4 FY2024.

In terms of GAAP, which includes a number of items left out by non-GAAP, WB posted a net loss attributable to shareholders of $4.72M or $0.02 per ADS, worse than the net profit of $8.87M or $0.04 per ADS a year ago. Note that the loss was mostly due to losses on investments and not on operations. Remember also that earnings in the final quarter of the fiscal tends to be lower as the end of the year brings certain accounting adjustments that need to be done before wrapping up the fiscal.

In all of FY2025, non-GAAP earnings totaled $1.65 per ADS on revenue of $1,757.22M, which is worse than in FY2024 when WB earned $1.82 on revenue of $1,754.68M. In terms of GAAP, though, WB earned $1.70 per ADS in FY2025, which is more than the $1.16 earned in FY2024, but this is primarily due to income from interest and investments.

Income from operations, which looks at the core business and leaves out changes on the non-operating side, declined from $494.32M in FY2024 to $464.83M in FY2025. Still, WB continues to be a quite profitable business with gross margin of 76% and operating margin of 26.2%. WB finished FY2025 with total debt of $1.86B, but this was more than offset by cash and cash equivalents totaling $2.3B.

The market was likely also disappointed by WB reducing its dividend. WB announced a cash dividend of $0.61 per ADS for the year along with the latest report. This is less than prior years when WB paid $0.82, $0.82 and $0.85 in 2025, 2024 and 2023, respectively, per ADS. On the other hand, the dividend still represents a very respectable yield of 7.1% with the stock last closing at $8.58, which compares very well in the tech space.

Valuations are lower

WB has more in its corner than just a higher-than-average dividend yield and an oversold stock close to support. The decrease in the stock price, which has seen the stock go from a high of $12.96 in October to $8.58 most recently, a decrease of about a third or so in six months, has lowered valuations to the point that getting in on WB is now significantly more palatable in terms of multiples.

For instance, TTM non-GAAP EPADS of $1.65 translates to a P/E ratio of 5.2x for WB, which compares well in the social media space where P/E ratios tend to be much higher. Valuations are also low in terms of the balance sheet. WB has a book value of $16.39 per ADS, which means WB is available at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.52x.

These multiples are well below what WB has tended to go for over the years. For instance, the average P/E in the last five years is 7.8x, well above the current 5.2x. Similarly, being valued at less than book value is unusual because on average WB has traded above it in the last five years. In other words, there is a sale going on.

What WB needs to work on

However, while there are reasons to be bullish on WB, which includes the stock close to support, multiples below the average and an above-average dividend yield, potential buyers of the stock may still be worried by the possibility earnings could come under further pressure and decrease like it did in the latest report.

For instance, while revenue grew, if only by a modest amount, operating income was reduced in both the final quarter and the year as a whole. This is because while revenue grew, cost of revenue grew even faster, as did most operating expenses. The latter includes product development, sales and marketing, but not general and administrative expenses.

WB needs to at the very least halt any decrease in earnings, and ideally grow it. To do this sustainably, revenue needs to grow faster than it has. WB needs more engagement from the users on the platform and ideally more users on the platform. To do this, WB has to come up with ways that gives people an incentive to turn to the platform and spend time on it.

It is worth mentioning that WB has been making a lot of changes in terms of how it operates internally, and these investments are in all likelihood driving up costs. For instance, WB has been working on search powered by AI and it is known from companies like Google that AI-enhanced search is more computationally intensive than regular search, which translates into higher cost.

The same is true for the recommendation engine WB has turned to in order to emulate the success companies like ByteDance have achieved with their recommendation engine. An interest-based feed using a recommendation engine will require more computations than a more traditional relationship-based feed, which means more spending on related infrastructure needed to make it all work.

The flip side of these investment is that while it can cost more in the short term, it could allow for faster growth in the long term, something WB needs since the top line has been rather flattish in recent years. Both AI search and the recommendation engine, for instance, have a lot of potential by increasing engagement to drive growth. It is possible WB is currently in a transition phase where the bottom line is reduced at first in order for the top line to grow faster, which will then drive the bottom line higher.

Why AI could have a lot of potential for WB

If WB is to succeed to boosting growth like it needs to, then AI is likely to be the main reason as it offers the best opportunities to do so. Recent developments in the AI world have revealed encouraging growth prospects WB could utilize. For instance, WB started out with text messages in its early years as the Chinese counterpart to what was Twitter, but the WB of the future could rely more heavily on video than it does text.

A number of AI tools have recently become available that can generate videos from scratch, which have really impressed users for their ability to generate realistic videos. Seedance 2.0 has stood out, but there are others. This is something that did not exist before, but which could cause users to flock to the platform to, for instance, share their AI-generated videos on WB. WB may not be growing all that fast at the moment, but with the way AI is opening up new capabilities that users did not have before, and with WB making the investments needed to take advantage, it could be different in the future.

Investor takeaways

WB has not done well recently and not without reason. The stock market has been getting hit by fighting in the Middle East, which has weighed on stocks. It does not help that WB released a soft report that featured reduced earnings. The dividend, which WB can count as among its strongest attributes, was also reduced.

However, the case for long WB has a number of arguments in its favor. The dividend even at the reduced rate is still better than most. The stock is oversold after the recent decline and close to a support level, which makes for a good time to enter in on WB. WB is virtually debt free with more cash than debt and a healthy balance sheet.

The income statement is more of an issue. While FY2025 saw an increase in GAAP earnings, this was the result of gains from investments and other income not related to operations. In terms of other metrics, the bottom line got worse. WB remains a profitable business overall with relatively high margins, but the decrease in operating income is something that must be reversed. This became even more so in the final quarter of the fiscal.

Nonetheless, there is reason for optimism. The decrease in earnings seems to be related to the investments WB has made to make the business grow faster. This includes a number of growth initiatives like AI search and a recommendation engine. The goal of all these initiatives, which tend to revolve around the use of AI, is to draw more people to the platform and/or get them to spend more time on the platform.

If this happens, revenue should grow faster than it has in recent years, which should help profits in the long run. In the short term, it will result in a hit to earnings as WB needs to open the purse and it may take time for growth initiatives to yield results. But it will be worth it if the result is faster growth for WB, something that is probably WB’s greatest weakness with its rather flattish growth.

I am therefore bullish on WB with everything stated earlier in mind. The recent earnings pressure is not something to be ignored, but WB deserves a chance at showing it can boost growth with its various growth initiatives. While earnings will get reduced as a consequence short term, it will be well worth it in my opinion if the result is a WB that becomes more dynamic with faster growth.

data.title: SoFi: Bulls Are Overlooking The Biggest Risk In The Story
data.description:
Back in January, I rated SoFi Technologies (SOFI) as a hold, as I was uncertain about the capital allocation strategy after the $3B raised across the July and December equity rounds.

The stock is down nearly 40%.

In the last earnings call, CFO Chris Lapointe didn't specify how they plan to spend the $3B. That said, his following remarks could be an indication that a broad acquisition spree is unlikely (which was my main concern heading into the Q4 print):

There's a lot of opportunities out there. I would say more than we've ever seen in eight years here. But I'd also say the bar is really high for us. When I say really high, we've looked at dozens and dozens of things, some of which were for sale, some of which were interesting to us. What we're prioritizing are things that can accelerate our growth versus the time it would take to build it ourselves.

Since my last coverage, I have been seeing a wave of contrarian investors and analysts who feel quite optimistic about the solid double-digit growth of the company.

In fact, the Street itself is projecting two more quarters of 30%+ revenue growth, followed by another two quarters with mid to high 20% growth.

That said, I prefer to remain a contrarian here and reiterate my hold rating. To be clear, the rationale has very little to do with valuation. In fact, I am not spooked by the 25x forward P/E multiple, even though it's significantly above the median in the financials sector.

What puts me off is the current macro backdrop, coupled with the earnings exposure of the company to its personal loan portfolio. My concern is that the current macroeconomic environment is pressuring the lower-end consumer.

The war in the Middle East is just half of the story. In fact, as I'm about to show in this article, there are early signs (very early, I have to admit) of leaving behind the highs of a credit cycle in personal loans.

My view could be wrong if the Strait of Hormuz reopens within the next few weeks, and the Fed starts cutting interest rates, starting in June. I believe there is a high chance for these two events to happen within that timeframe, which is the main reason I am not rating SoFi with a sell rating.

Below, I discuss more.

A Broad-Based Selloff In Credit Services

Let's put SoFi's selloff into context. The company is operating within the credit services industry, inside the broader financial services sector.

Take a look at the 3-month returns of the constituents within this industry:

The predominance of red tells me something: part of the selloff is driven by a risk-off move, external to the company.

Why?

Trump's call for a one-year 10% cap on credit-card interest rates starting January 20. I remember that day very well, as I was looking at the financials sector, and I could see a lot of red tiles in the heatmap visual above coming from stocks like Synchrony, Bread Financial, and Capital One falling between 8% and 11%. In fact, even Visa and Mastercard slipped 1.8% each.

Funny enough, by January 20, the overhang still had not cleared. In fact, the 10% cap is still not implemented today, March 31. Overall, back in January, analysts doubted the cap could be imposed without Congress, and I lean on the same view.

That said, a headline risk is a risk nonetheless, so one should consider this downside catalyst in their risk assessment for any company within the credit services industry.

A Few Headlines That Raised My Eyebrow

On March 30, Reuters reported that Robinhood and SoFi both pitched for roles in the retail distribution of a potential SpaceX IPO share sale in the U.S.

For reference, it is well known that SpaceX is targeting a late-2026 IPO, with valuations that go as far as $1T after the xAI merger. In fact, some sources are floating figures as high as $1.5T.

However, it appears that E*Trade was in talks to lead the IPO, and some sources suggest that SpaceX may be considering cutting Robinhood and SoFi out, though discussions are still ongoing.

It's a long shot, however, if SpaceX finally decides to lead its IPO with SoFi, that could be a headline to turn around the pessimism surrounding this stock.

Speaking of pessimism, it seems that Muddy Waters’ SoFi short campaign is gaining momentum after a recent short thesis published on March 30. That comes on top of their March 21 and March 17 reports.

What I found particularly interesting after these short reports was the reaction from the CEO. On March 17, CEO Anthony Noto filed a Form 4 showing an open-market purchase of 28,900 shares at a weighted-average price of $17.3189.

That's equivalent to a $500k million purchase. Not bad, although the stock is down 13.6% since his insider buying activity.

On top of that, he was the only one who bought in March. I would have loved to see a cluster of insider buying activity this month. Instead, one of the EVPs sold 3% of his position on March 20:

Why I'm Not Buying The Dip

It seems that I am not the only one. Back on March 19, Wells Fargo initiated coverage on SoFi with an equal weight rating, citing a price target of $19.

Back in January, BofA maintained an underperform rating, citing limited upside at the current multiple even after SoFi’s capital raise (a topic I discussed in my last coverage).

My discontent is with the high concentration of unsecured personal loans.

Let me explain why.

Personal loans represent balance sheet exposure. Therefore, any credit deterioration can hit revenue, fair-value marks, and capital generation.

My concern is that the consumer is now being pressured by high gas prices due to the Hormuz blockade. In fact, recent headlines like this one make me sweat:

If Hormuz is not reopened, the U.S. consumer will have to deal with higher gas prices for longer. For the consumers at the lower end of the K-shaped curve, that could mean defaulting on some of their loan payments.

In the last earnings release, personal-loan annualized charge-offs increased to 2.80% in Q4 from 2.60% in Q3. On top of that, the weighted-average annual default assumption on personal loans increased to 4.46% from 4.33% in the prior quarter.

As seen above, there is a big gap in that metric vs the student loan portfolio.

Again, it's the headlines that make me sweat. I include below one from February:

It seems that subprime loans are growing in the U.S., with some reports suggesting that 26.4 million consumers carried those loans as of the end of December last year, up from 24.5 million a year earlier.

On top of that, the New York Fed’s February 2026 household-debt update showed student-loan stress worsening, with 9.6% of balances 90+ days delinquent.

Even though I want to believe that SoFi has a very healthy personal loan base, these headlines only deteriorate the investor confidence in the credit services industry. Therefore, I don't see how the company will be able to turnaroud the poor sentiment surrounding its stock.

Looking Ahead

Since the highs in November last year, the stock has been down over 50%. That said, valuation still looks stretched. As I'm typing, investors are paying today 25x for the company's next year's earnings. For reference, the median of the financials sector is 9.6x.

Overall, aside from a long shot to participate in SpaceX's upcoming IPO, I don't see any compelling catalyst that would turn around the pessimism surrounding this stock (and also, surrounding the credit services industry).

Overall, over the last 3-months, I noticed a broader risk-off move in this industry, and given the recent headlines and the current pressures of higher gas prices for lower-end consumers, I am increasingly concerned.

From a technical perspective, the next support level appears to be at $10. That's a $5 haircut from today's $15.1 share price.

Overall, I prefer to remain on the sidelines until the sentiment in this industry improves, likely in the second half of this year, if (big if) Hormuz reopens.

data.title: Don't Be Fooled By The Average: IBHF's Real Yield Story
data.description:
The iShares iBonds 2026 Tеrm High Yield and Incоme ETF (IBHF) is a spеcific type of bond ETF. Unlikе traditional bond ETFs that roll оver their hоldings indefinitely, IBHF has a fixеd mаturity dаte. All hоldings mаture within thе samе calеndar yеar, aftеr which thе fund is liquidаted and rеturns cаpital to investors — similar to hоlding аn individual bоnd directly. This structurе makes thеm attrаctive to invеstors whо wаnt the divеrsification of а fund but the prеdictability of а fixеd mаturity. iShаres iBоnds 2026 Term High Yield and Income ETF was issued on 11/10/2020. At the time of writing, the fund has close to $1.04 billion in assets under management. Its management fee is close to 0.35% (expense ratio 0.35%). The 5-year total return on NAV is close to 4.57%, and the 3-year total return is around 8.67%. As of 03/27/2026, there are 44,660,000 shares outstanding.

Holdings

IBHF has 241 holdings at the time of writing. The weighted average coupon of all is around 4.27%, and the effective duration is close to 0.38 years. The largest part of the fund is 16.07% in the "Consumer Cyclical" sector, 12.89% in the "Finance Companies," and 10.77% in "Communications."

Almost all of the holdings (approximately 96.84%) have a maturity between 0 and 1 year, as you can see in the picture below. The weighted average maturity of IBHF is close to 0.39 years. 2.64% of the fund is in cash and derivatives.

In the next screenshot, the credit quality of the fund is shown. The biggest percentages are in BBB-rated (34.02%) and BB-rated (45.79%). 7.56% of the holdings are B-rated, and 8.58% are CCC-rated.

And here are the top 10 holdings of the fund:

IBHF's portfolio has an average weighted YTM of ~7.54%, which looks attractive for a short-term instrument with a maturity of 2026. Here we come to the main thesis of the article. This percentage is actually not 'real' - it is heavily distorted by a handful of holdings. In the following table, we have divided the ratio of holdings by the respective yield.

You can see for yourself that the largest part of the fund - close to 67%, or 161 holdings (with а total weight 33%) are with a yield to maturity below 5%. 22% are with yield to maturity between 5-6% аnd a total weight of 36.90%. The rest, 11%, are with yields above 6%. Only 3% of the fund has a yield to maturity above 15%. Below are shown all holdings of IBHF - you can see how the high-yield ones stand out significantly:

Thе mеdiаn weighted YTM is only аround 5.46%. Literаlly 70.9% of the pоrtfoliо carries a YTM bеlоw 6%, аnd 80.5% of the аssets are cоncentratеd in the 4–7% range, with a rеal averаge YTM of just 5.28%. Only 10 cоmpanies with а YTM аbove 10% hаve a wеight of approximately 8.5% аnd аlone cоntribute 2.54% of the tоtal 7.54% YTM, оr сlose to 33.7% оf the entirе avеrage YTM cоmеs from lеss thаn 9% of the pоrtfolio.

In the chart are shown the high-yield holdings that falsely raise the average YTM:

Many of them are with "Junk" credit ratings and are trading below par, even thоugh thеir mаturity is оnly mоnths аwаy—thе mаrket is еxprеssing sеrious dоubt thаt thеy will pаy оut thеir full vаluе.

But this is only a small part of the fund. In reality, 80% of IBHF's holdings are safer, with yields to maturity of between 4% and 7%, and are with higher credit ratings. With a real YTM of 5.28%, compared to the 6-month treasury yield, the spread is only close to 1.2% — for a high-yield fund with credit risk, this is a very tight compensation.

Since IBHF effectively behaves like a bond but carries a real average YTM of around 5%, investors may want to consider alternatives — investment-grade baby bonds trading below par, which offer higher yields with lower credit risk:

Conclusion

8.5% of the IBHF portfolio is 'dirty' and artificially inflates the average YTM by almost 2 full percentage points. The investor is buying something that looks like 7.54% but is actually 5.1% YTM. If yоu want to take risks and you are an investor who consciously wants distressed bonds, yоu cаn buy Hughеs Satellite or Baffinland directly. If yоu don't wаnt to risk, yоu're better off bеtting on аnоther ETF, аs there аre many аlternatives on thе mаrket for over 5%.
